text: 10 Large Language Models (LLMs) have been applied to solve a wide spectrum of difficult problems that range from basic quantitative abilities to high-level communication. These models suffer from confabulations (or hallucinations) that lead to producing reasonably-sounding yet false statements that have been denoted as hallucinations in the literature (Bang et al., 2023; Borji, 2023). Most current LLMs fail to conduct real scientific discovery. Here, we describe the first-ever FunSearch (short for searching in the function space) evolutionary procedure for coupled a LLM with a programmatic evaluator. We show by several demonstrations how FunSearch leads to advances on several record results for LLMs (Lehman et al., 2022). More importantly, using FunSearch we improved the state-of-the-art record in one of the most famous and basic extremal combinatorics (XTC) open problems, known as the cap set problem for finite dimensional sets (CSP); we also showed how new asymptotic cap set records can be computed. In other words, we provided the first-ever discoveries regarding an important, open problem, which were achieved using a Large Language Model; a feat that we are not aware has ever been done in the past for well established open problems, and that we demonstrate quantitatively here. We also showed in a completely different setting, namely algorithmic computer science, the capabilities of the approach by using FunSearch to improve many state-of-the-art heuristics for a well studied and practical bin packing problem which is also the classical problem underlying storage and packing in cloud object storage for Bin/Pack, which had never been properly approached in the optimization literature. FunSearch gives a method that for the first time leads to improvements and/or records on actual open problems and also beyond. All prior methods in computer search deal with searching and exploring solutions, meaning that they search for instances of the solutions in the solution space while FunSearch rather searches for the representations of the solutions in the function space; it finds a “how to solve this” rather than just a “what is the solution.” A functional representation is also considerably more human and natural to interpret for any given solution. An important property of the FunSearch procedure in this regard, is that it affords the possibility of utilizing human insights at two levels of the computational procedure that give an opportunity for true feedback loops between humans and the black/white box procedures provided by the computer searches; such feedback loops are also a prerequisite in order to achieve successful deployment of any final solutions found and suggested by FunSearch.
explanation: The text uses grandiose claims about open problems and exhibits inconsistent terminology (e.g., XTC, CSP) and awkward phrasing common in AI-generated prose. These cues, along with dense citations and forward-looking statements, suggest a higher likelihood of synthetic origin.
